elements.sent-2411-001: Cardiovascular surgeries and mechanical circulatory support devices create non-physiological blood flow conditions that can be detrimental, especially for pediatric patients.
elements.sent-2411-002: A source of complications is mechanical red blood cell (RBC) damage induced by the localized supraphysiological shear fields.
elements.sent-2411-003: To understand such complications, we introduce a multi-scale numerical model to predict the risk of hemolysis in a set of idealized anatomies.
elements.sent-2411-004: We employed our in-house CFD solver coupled with Lagrangian tracking and cell-resolved fluid-structure interaction to measure flow-induced stresses and strains on the RBC membrane.
elements.sent-2411-005: The Norwood procedure, well-known to be associated with high mortality rate, is selected for its importance in the survival of the single-ventricle population.
elements.sent-2411-006: We simulated three anatomies including 2.5mm and 4.0mm diameter modified Blalock-Taussig (BT) shunts and a 2.5mm central shunt (CS), with hundreds of RBCs in each case for statistical analysis.
elements.sent-2411-007: The results show that the conditions created by these surgeries can elongate RBCs by more than two-fold (3.1\% of RBCs for 2.5mm BT shunt, 1.4\% for 4mm BT shunt, and 8.8\% for CS).
elements.sent-2411-008: Shear and areal strain metrics also reveal that the central shunt creates the greatest deformations on the RBCs membrane, indicating it is a more hemolytic procedure in comparison to the BT shunt.
elements.sent-2411-009: Between the two BT shunts, the smaller diameter is slightly more prone to hemolysis.
elements.sent-2411-010: These conclusions are confirmed when strain history and different damage thresholds are considered.
elements.sent-2411-011: The spatial damage maps produced based on these metrics highlighted hot zones that match the clinical images of shunt thrombosis.
elements.sent-2411-012: Keywords: Cell-resolved simulations, Hemolysis quantification, Damage maps, Single ventricles, Stage-one operation, Fluid-structure interaction, Blood flow modeling.
elements.sent-2411-013: Hypoplastic left heart syndrome (HLHS) is a critical congenital heart defect where the left side of the heart is underdeveloped.
elements.sent-2411-014: Patients with this malformation suffer from a range of conditions including atresia or stenosis of mitral and aortic valves, and hypoplasia of the ascending aorta, aortic arch, and left ventricle \cite{Noonan1958 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: In the United States, more than 1 in every 4000 neonates are born with HLHS \cite{Mai2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Infants born with this fatal defect require multiple surgeries for survival.
elements.sent-2411-017: The Norwood (or stage I) procedure is a palliative surgery performed on newborns with HLHS as well as other complex single ventricle defects that share the same physiologic features \cite{Jacobs1995 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: This operation is often followed by Glenn (or stage II) and Fontan (or stage III) operations, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-019: During the Norwood operation, a new aorta is created and connected to the right ventricle, and a shunt connecting the new aorta to pulmonary arteries is inserted to maintain the blood flow to the lungs.
elements.sent-2411-020: Modified Blalock–Taussig (BT) shunt, central shunt, and Sano shunt also called right ventricle to pulmonary artery (RV-PA) shunt, are different shunt configurations used in the stage I procedure.
elements.sent-2411-021: All these configurations are inherently flawed as they create a parallel circulation between systemic and pulmonary circuits that overloads the single ventricle.
elements.sent-2411-022: Furthermore, they produce hypoxia due to the mixture of oxygenated and de-oxygenated blood in the right atrium.
elements.sent-2411-023: Complications such as renal failure \cite{Chamberlain2022 , blockage of the shunt by means of clot formation or thrombosis, cardiac arrest \cite{Rajab2021 , and blood trauma are also common among Norwood patients.}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Thus, despite improvements since the introduction of this procedure in 1983, the in-hospital mortality rate remains as high as approximately 16\% in the Norwood population \cite{Mascio2019, Murni2019, Meza2023 ; which is among the greatest in common congenital heart procedures.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Thus, there exists a pressing need to enhance the survival rate of the Norwood procedure, as the first stage in the treatment of HLHS.
elements.sent-2411-026: Some of the mortality in Norwood patients can be attributed to the blockage of the shunt, which is caused by the abnormal flow environment within the shunt.
elements.sent-2411-027: Hemolysis has been also observed secondary to Norwood procedure with modified BT shunt reducing patient's hematocrit from 55\% to 37\% within two weeks after the operation \cite{Ryerson2007 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Accumulating evidence suggests that mechanical RBC damage, i.e., blood trauma, might be the central underlying reason for secondary complications \cite{McNamee2023 such as renal and liver failure, thrombosis, and exacerbated hypoxia.}
elements.sent-2411-029: RBC damage and destruction, increase the risk of thrombosis by promoting clotting pathways, e.g., von Willebrand factor (vWF) and platelet activation \cite{Faghih2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Damage and loss of healthy RBCs also further reduce oxygen delivery.
elements.sent-2411-031: Even without complete rupture, and through the pores formed on the membrane of highly deformed RBC, hemoglobin and other RBC contents can be released to plasma.
elements.sent-2411-032: Free hemoglobin is toxic and can lead to acute kidney injury \cite{DVANAJSCAK20191400 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: However, these processes are highly complicated to model computationally.
elements.sent-2411-034: As a result, to gain some insight into the effect of shunt flow environment on the blood constituents, we take a preliminary step and look at RBC response to altered flow in this study as it pertains to other mentioned complications.
elements.sent-2411-035: Blood is a colloidal suspension of cells immersed in plasma.
elements.sent-2411-036: The cellular nature of blood is the underlying reason for many of its fascinating characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-037: The average hematocrit (Ht), i.e., the volume fraction of RBCs (erythrocytes), is roughly 45\%; White blood cells and platelets combined form around 1\% of blood's volume and the remaining is plasma \cite{DeHaan2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Hence, the properties of RBCs, the most numerous of blood cells, dictate the behavior of blood.
elements.sent-2411-039: Red blood cells have a biconcave disk shape with an approximate diameter of $8 \mu m$, and thickness of $2 \mu m$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-040: When RBCs are exposed to high non-physiologically hemodynamic stresses, such as those created with reconstructive surgeries and in medical devices, they might rupture in a process called hemolysis.
elements.sent-2411-041: This lethal form of blood damage is usually purposefully averted in the recent generation of circulatory assist devices thanks to our understanding of the underlying reasons, i.e., higher than normal magnitudes of and prolonged exposure to shear stress.
elements.sent-2411-042: Nonetheless, circumventing such extreme conditions is not adequate.
elements.sent-2411-043: As even mechanical stresses well below the identified limits for hemolysis, are strong enough to cause structural and functional alterations to RBC, namely sublethal RBC damage \cite{McNamee2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Unlike hemolysis that can be easily identified by in vitro and in vivo studies through measuring the concentration of plasma free hemoglobin, detecting sublethal blood trauma is difficult due to the lack of a direct test \cite{Olia2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Conversely, computational fluid dynamics (CFD) enable us to not only simulate the global transport of blood flow, but also, microscopic phenomena at the cellular level, such as RBC deformation, aggregation, and coagulation, to gain valuable insight into the challenges associated with blood-wetted devices and cardiovascular surgeries.
elements.sent-2411-046: In light of this, cell-resolved computational techniques have been developed and used to replicate blood cell dynamics, albeit for small-scale (sub-millimeter) simulations.
elements.sent-2411-047: Dissipative particle dynamics (DPD) \cite{Fedosov2010,Liu2024 , lattice Boltzmann method (LBM), and smoothed particle hydrodynamics (SPH) \cite{Hosseini2012 are particle-based methods \cite{Tsubota2006 generally used for simulation of RBCs.}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: For instance, Javadi et al. \cite{Javadi2021 utilized DPD to study the effect of different biophysical factors on blood's viscosity in a cubic simulation box of edge size equal to $50 \mu m$, where they were able to simulate up to $Ht = 55\%$.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Takeishi et al. \cite{Takeishi2019 numerically analyzed the rheology of a suspension of RBCs in blood plasma for wall-bounded shear flows using LBM coupled with IBM.}
elements.sent-2411-050: For a domain size of $\approx 10 \mu m$ in each direction, they solved for volume fractions up to $41\%$.
elements.sent-2411-051: As opposed to the meshless particle-based methods that have to fill the whole space with particles, immersed boundary methods (IBM) benefit from a combination of CFD grids and a collection of immersed particles representing an immersed boundary; which have been used for the simulation of blood cells and platelets and understanding their behavior \cite{Yazdani2011, Kassen2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-052: On the other hand, boundary integral methods are another type of continuum models that only solve for the velocity of a surface such as a blood cell membrane.
elements.sent-2411-053: Once the membrane velocity is obtained, one can calculate the solution at any point inside or outside of the closed membrane, if required.
elements.sent-2411-054: Assumptions of thin membrane and Stokes flow in the vicinity of blood cells permit the use of boundary element methods which is considerably cheaper compared to other methods.
elements.sent-2411-055: This method, which is adopted here, has been used in several earlier studies \cite{Ramanujan1998, Pozrikidis2003, Zhao2010, Sinha2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-056: For example, Pozrikidis simulated the flow-induced deformation of RBC under simple shear flow \cite{Pozrikidis2003 .}
elements.sent-2411-057: In another study, Zhao et al. \cite{Zhao2010 utilized the same method to capture the deformation of RBCs in complex geometries with spectral accuracy facilitated by the use of spherical harmonics.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Also, different modes of RBC motion have been well studied with the use of boundary element methods \cite{Sinha2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-059: Fully coupled cell-resolved simulation of RBC deformation and damage in organ-scale flow is prohibitively expensive due to the orders of magnitude difference between the size of RBCs and arteries.
elements.sent-2411-060: It is for this reason that blood cell damage in large-scale flows has been historically predicted using stress-based models in conjunction with empirical equations (e.g., \cite{Li2022, Giersiepen1990 ).}
elements.sent-2411-061: As opposed to stress-based models that rely only on instantaneous stresses of flow, strain-based models coupled with Lagrangian particle tracking methods account for RBC motion and deformation to give a rather accurate prediction of blood damage in large-scale flows.
elements.sent-2411-062: Arora et al. \cite{Arora2004 first proposed the idea of accounting for RBC deformation along their path lines to estimate RBC damage through a tensor-based, i.e., strain-based, model.}
elements.sent-2411-063: They simulated RBCs as ellipsoidal liquid droplets and employed their method to study blood trauma in a 2D pump.
elements.sent-2411-064: More recently, the same idea was utilized and improved to resolve the shape of a handful of blood cells in 3D geometries such as a narrow neck \cite{Zhao2010 and mechanical prostheses heart valve \cite{Ezzeldin2015 , and in 3D turbulent flows \cite{Rydquist2024 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: Moreover, reduced-order models of RBCs \cite{Porcaro2023,Porcaro2024 have been recently developed to approximately obtain RBC deformation at a lower cost compared to cell-resolved models.}
elements.sent-2411-066: However, these methods cannot fully replicate the intricate cellular-level physics.
elements.sent-2411-067: To avoid the high cost of direct numerical simulation of RBCs in large-scale flows, we use a multi-scale cell-resolved Lagrangian model to produce cell-scale accurate measures of RBC deformation in large-scale flow.
elements.sent-2411-068: The results of such simulation provide insight into mechanical load on cells that can be used for relative comparison of different geometries \cite{Gusenbauer2018,Porcaro2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-069: The goal of this study, thus, is to obtain a reliable statistical description of the cell-scale mechanical behavior of red blood cells in different designs for the Norwood procedure.
elements.sent-2411-070: For this purpose, we consider modified BT shunt (mBTS) and central shunt (CS) and compare them in terms of various mechanical factors that are linked to sublethal blood trauma.
elements.sent-2411-071: It should be noted that the overall computational framework that is utilized here is generic and can be applied to other cardiovascular surgeries and devices to compare them in terms of in-bulk RBC damage due to medium flow-induced stresses.
elements.sent-2411-072: Moreover, the methodology is designed with special attention paid to limiting the overall computational time which would be beneficial for individual risk assessment by building digital twins.
elements.sent-2411-073: The paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-074: The second section covers the description and validation of the numerical methods used for the simulation of the flow field, tracking of red blood cells, and blood cell membrane dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-075: The third section is dedicated to the independence studies of numerical parameters along with the choice of material properties.
elements.sent-2411-076: The main results and their interpretation are presented in the fourth section.
elements.sent-2411-077: Finally, the main conclusions of this study are reported in the fifth section, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-078: The present section introduces the modeling approach, procedures, and governing equations.
elements.sent-2411-079: Moreover, the validation of the new components of the solver is covered in this section.
elements.sent-2411-080: Direct simulation of RBCs in organ-scale flows is prohibitively expensive due to multiple order separation of scales between blood cells and organs as observed in Fig. \ref{fig:Scale .}
elements.sent-2411-081: Hence, a multi-scale cell-resolved approach, introduced in a previous work \cite{Rydquist2022 , is adapted here, where a one-way coupled approach is used between RBCs and surrounding flow.}
elements.sent-2411-082: This means that the effect of flow on RBCs is simulated while the effect of individual RBCs on macro-scale flow is taken into account using effective medium properties, representing whole blood.
elements.sent-2411-083: The accuracy of this modeling approach has been demonstrated in an earlier study, which showed simulating a single cell using an effective viscosity accurately reproduces results obtained from simulation of whole blood, thereby effectively capturing the RBCs interactions through an adjustment of plasma viscosity \cite{Rydquist2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-084: The RBCs are much smaller than the characteristic length of large-scale flow and almost immediately relax to the velocity of surrounding flow due to their short relaxation time.
elements.sent-2411-085: Thus, the far-field boundary condition imposed at the cell-scale is obtained from the macro-scale flow field according to \begin{equation}\bm{u}^\infty(\bm{x},t) = \bm{U}(\bm{x}_c,t) + (\bm{x}-\bm{x}_c)\cdot\nabla \bm{u}^\infty(\bm{x}_c,t), \label{eqn:decompose}\end{equation} where $\bm{u ^\infty$ is the far field fluid velocity, $\bm{U}$ is the fluid velocity at the location of RBC (i.e., its center point), $\nabla \bm{u}$ is the velocity gradient, and $\bm{x _c$ is the cell location.}}
elements.sent-2411-086: This means that we assume the flow curvature, before disturbance introduced by the RBC, is negligible at the length scale of a RBC.
elements.sent-2411-087: Hence, RBC follows the flow with uniform velocity, while the velocity gradient part of the flow field deforms the cell.
elements.sent-2411-088: From a computational standpoint, three separate solvers are utilized in this study for the multi-scale cell-resolved simulation of RBCs.
elements.sent-2411-089: First, CFD simulation of large-scale flow is done with the help of a lumped parameter network (LPN).
elements.sent-2411-090: This is to resolve blood flow behavior at the anatomical level.
elements.sent-2411-091: Second, a Lagrangian particle tracking algorithm specifically designed for fast simulation of particles in time-periodic flows is introduced and used to track red blood cells and acquire velocity gradient data along their trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-092: Last but not least, the deformation of the membrane of red blood cells due to the fluid-structure interaction (FSI) with the fluid inside and outside of the membrane is captured using a boundary integral method (BIM).
elements.sent-2411-093: The flow chart of all utilized methods, data input/output, and a simplified visualization of the outcomes of this multi-scale approach which is the deformation of red blood cells tracked through an organ-scale flow are illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:FlowChart .}
elements.sent-2411-094: In the following sections, we discuss each of these three components in detail.

elements.sent-2411-098: Equations 2-6 are solved using a verified in-house multi-physics finite element solver (MUPFES) \cite{EsmailyMoghadam2013,Esmaily-Moghadam2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-099: This solver utilizes upwinding and pressure-stabilization techniques to ensure the stability of result in strong convection and permit the use of equal order shape functions for velocity and pressure \cite{BROOKS1982,Jia2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-100: A specialized iterative algorithm, preconditioner, and parallelization strategy are implemented for an efficient and scalable solution of the linear system of equations \cite{Esmaily-Moghadam2013,Esmaily_Partition2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-101: The solver uses an implicit generalized-$\alpha$ time integration scheme \cite{JANSEN2000305 , where at each time step, several Newton–Raphson iterations are performed to drop the residual by over four orders of magnitude.}
elements.sent-2411-102: At each Newton–Raphson iteration, a linear system is solved using the generalized minimal residual (GMRES) method \cite{SaadGMRES .}
elements.sent-2411-103: This solver is parallelized using a message-passing interface (MPI) using domain partitioning.
elements.sent-2411-104: All computations are performed on a cluster of AMD Opteron$^{TM}$ 6378 processors that are interconnected via a QDR Infiniband.
elements.sent-2411-105: The idealized geometries adopted for macro-scale blood flow simulations are based on earlier studies involving Norwood operation \cite{Jia2021,Esmaily-Moghadam2015,Moghadam2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-106: The adopted anatomy and direction of blood flow in various branches are shown in Fig. \ref{fig:LabeledBT (a).}
elements.sent-2411-107: Briefly, blood flow coming from the heart enters the circulatory system from ascending aorta (AoA).
elements.sent-2411-108: This flow goes toward the lower body through the descending aorta (AoD), and toward the upper body through the brachiocephalic artery (BA), right common carotid artery (RCCA), left common carotid artery (LCCA), and left subclavian artery (LSA).
elements.sent-2411-109: Flows from the upper and lower parts of the body will return to the heart before reentering AoA.
elements.sent-2411-110: The insertion of BT or central shunt, allows blood flow to go toward the lungs through the right pulmonary artery (RPA) and left pulmonary artery (LPA), before returning to the heart.
elements.sent-2411-111: The right coronary artery (RCA) is responsible for supplying blood to the heart muscles.

elements.sent-2411-116: In brief, to capture the complex response of the circulatory system, a system of ordinary differential equations (ODEs), is solved in the 0D domain to compute the pressure and flow rate for the Neumann and Dirichlet BCs in the 3D domain.
elements.sent-2411-117: The components appearing in the LPN, using an electrical circuit analogy (taking flow and pressure as current and voltage, respectively), are resistors, capacitors, inductors, and diodes to recreate physiological behaviors such as resistance to flow, vessel wall distensibility, blood flow inertia, and heart valves, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-118: We use both Dirichlet and Neumann BCs at the 0D/3D interface.
elements.sent-2411-119: The Dirichlet BC, which is imposed on AoA, requires passing pressure data to the 0D solver in exchange for receiving flow rate data.
elements.sent-2411-120: In contrast, the Neumann BC, which is imposed on remaining branches, requires passing the flow rate to the 0D solver in exchange for the pressure.
elements.sent-2411-121: The values of different parameters of this LPN are reported in Appendix \ref{appendix:LPN .}
elements.sent-2411-122: These values recreate the working condition of a newborn's heart after the Norwood operation with 120 beats per minute.
elements.sent-2411-123: We use tetrahedral elements for discretizing the solution (both velocity and pressure) in space.
elements.sent-2411-124: To ensure grid-independency of our results while keeping these computations affordable, we perform successive mesh adaptation.

elements.sent-2411-138: To reduce this particle tracking cost, we adopt a search-box algorithm, which is explained in previous work in detail \cite{Rydquist2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-139: In brief, at the beginning of the particle tracking simulation, a coarse structured Cartesian grid, referred to as search boxes, is built on top of the CFD mesh, and elements overlapping with each search box are identified.
elements.sent-2411-140: After this initial step, at each time step, the element hosting a specific particle is found by searching only through the elements associated with the easy-to-identify associated search box.
elements.sent-2411-141: Note that as explained in \cite{Rydquist2020 , there exists an optimal number of search boxes based on factors such as number of particles ($N_p$), number of elements ($N_e$), number of time steps ($N_t$), element type, etc. that is adopted in this study.}
elements.sent-2411-142: A second factor influencing the cost of particle tracking is the unsteady nature of the flow.
elements.sent-2411-143: Through the cardiac cycle, heart muscles relax and contract periodically during the diastole and systole phases, respectively, which creates a time-periodic flow inside the veins.
elements.sent-2411-144: Therefore, particle tracking is done as a post-process step by incorporating the periodicity of the flow into the solver which further reduces the computational cost of the particle tracking algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-145: Instead of simultaneously solving for the flow field and particles during multiple cardiac cycles, the flow field can be obtained for a single cycle before starting the particle tracking algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-146: Once the flow field is obtained, we can use the generated data to track particles going through our desired geometry for multiple cardiac cycles and simulation instances without having to solve for the flow field over and over again.
elements.sent-2411-147: In practice, CFD results are first saved, albeit every few time steps.
elements.sent-2411-148: The gap between time steps is selected to preserve temporal fluctuations in the flow field.
elements.sent-2411-149: This way we avoid reading too many large data files, which is highly time and memory-consuming.
elements.sent-2411-150: To map this coarse sampling of data to finer time gaps, we use the fast Fourier transform (FFT).
elements.sent-2411-151: We verify that the original CFD results are preserved with a downsampling rate of two, with a maximum error of less than 2\%, which is adopted in this study.
elements.sent-2411-152: The validation of the implemented particle tracking algorithm is done using a Hagen–Poiseuille flow, for which the analytical solution is known.
elements.sent-2411-153: Particles' velocity obtained by the solver is compared against the analytical solution; The maximum error among 10000 particles for this case is $0.1\%$, proving the correctness of the implemented algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-154: Particles are introduced into the computational domain through the AoA inlet via a random seeding algorithm that respects the local fluid velocity.
elements.sent-2411-155: Namely, this algorithm seeds more particles per unit time in regions of the inlet where the flow is entering the domain faster.

elements.sent-2411-350: Earlier experimental studies reported that extensive rupture of RBCs happens after a critical shear rate of around 42,000 s$^{-1}$ \cite{Leverett1972 .}

elements.sent-2411-352: For instance, area dilatation of 6\% is numerically observed for RBCs subjected to shear flow as the shear rate approaches its critical limit in different studies \cite{Vitale,Sohrabi2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-353: Razizadeh et al. \cite{Razizadeh2020 studied molecular dynamics of pore formation, growth, and recovery, for RBC membranes under large deformation.}
elements.sent-2411-354: They showed that under large deformations pores will form on RBCs membrane and there exists a critical areal strain above which the pores will start forming.
elements.sent-2411-355: The onset of this mechanical pore formation is found to be 10.2\% and 12.5\% areal strains for two lipid bi-layers attached to cytoskeletons with junction-to-junction distances similar to that of the RBCs and twice denser cytoskeleton compared to that of the RBC at its normal state.
elements.sent-2411-356: Moreover, it has been shown that elongation of RBCs can cause structural damage \cite{Mohandas1994, Evans1975 , with critical shear strain $\lambda_1/\lambda_2$ being 2.4 to 3.}

elements.sent-2411-358: Since higher elongations were not observed, this range is considered to be the critical elongation after which cells rupture.

elements.sent-2411-395: An alternative way to account for the history of cell deformation is to rely on a cell damage index (CDI), which is defined based on the cumulative deviation of RBC area \cite{Gusenbauer2018 as \begin{equation}CDI = \frac{1}{n_{RBC} }\sum_{id=1}^{n_{RBC}} \sum_i \left( \frac{ |A_{id}(t_i) - A_0|}{A_0} \right)^\alpha \Delta t_i. \label{eqn:cdi}\end{equation} $\alpha$ in this relation controls the importance of extreme events such that $\alpha > 1$ assigns more weight to instances of larger deformation in determining the damage.}

elements.sent-2411-406: Thus, relative cell damage indices using different values of this exponent are reported in Table \ref{table:CDI for damage criteria that are defined based on elongation, shear strain, and area expansion.}

elements.sent-2411-407: The first observation based on these results is that regardless of the utilized exponent and criterion, 4.0mm mBTS is always a safer choice compared to 2.5mm mBTS.
elements.sent-2411-408: Secondly, while all CDIs suggest that the 2.5mBTS is more damaging compared to the others at $\alpha=1$, increasing the exponent results in 2.5CS having the highest CDIs followed by the 2.5BT, and 4.0BT, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-409: Lastly, the $\alpha$ at which a given level of damage is predicted varies between different criteria.
elements.sent-2411-410: For instance for $\alpha=2.5$ and area expansion, $CDI_{\rm 2.5CS}$ is 9\% larger than that of 2.5BT, and $CDI_{\rm 4.0TS}$ is 39\% smaller compared to 2.5BT.
elements.sent-2411-411: These figures are 20\% and 21\% for instance when considering $\alpha= 4.0$ and shear strain instead.
elements.sent-2411-412: Thus, the relative destructiveness of instances of larger deformation compared to lower ones for each criterion (the $\alpha$ exponent), should be studied and measured experimentally before applying CDI to obtain a quantitative measure of damage.
elements.sent-2411-413: It should be noted that the studied anatomies are only compared based on the relative red cell damage in this study.
elements.sent-2411-414: Other factors in the choice of a design such as the heart load, oxygen delivery, thrombus formation, etc., must be considered before a choice is made.
elements.sent-2411-415: Furthermore, a more direct link must be established between the mechanical stresses and strains applied to RBCs and the sublethal RBC damage and hemolysis.
elements.sent-2411-416: Therefore, even though the conclusion of this study may remain valid for comparison between various reconstructive surgeries or blood-wetted devices on a relative basis, the results cannot be used as a quantitative basis for evaluating the absolute risk of an operation for direct comparison against experimental hemolysis index yet.
elements.sent-2411-417: Moreover, idealized geometries are utilized in this study for a general comparison of different surgical configurations.
elements.sent-2411-418: Patient-specific anatomical models will be required to produce more accurate predictions and tailored surgical planning for individual patients.
elements.sent-2411-419: In line with the need for better surgical options for pediatric patients with severe heart defects like HLHS, this work conducts a multi-scale cell-resolved in silico study of flow-induced red blood cell deformation as a consequence of altered organ-scale blood flow paths in Norwood or stage I procedure.
elements.sent-2411-420: RBC damage is selected due to its connection with post-operative complications observed in these patients.
elements.sent-2411-421: Two different shunt configurations, i.e., Blalock-Taussig shunt and central shunt, were simulated and compared.
elements.sent-2411-422: Moreover, the effect of shunt size was studied by considering 2.5mm and 4.0mm modified BT shunts.
elements.sent-2411-423: These surgical options were compared in terms of various deformation statistics as well as spatial damage maps, which allowed for the identification of hot zones in each configuration.
elements.sent-2411-424: The central shunt configuration produces more incidents in which RBCs undergo extreme deformation.
elements.sent-2411-425: Between the two mBTS sizes, it was the smaller diameter that produced those extreme events more frequently, but only sparingly.
elements.sent-2411-426: We hypothesized that those incidents, which can damage RBCs, are caused by the steep pressure gradient within the central shunt that in turn exposes RBCs to a high level of shear.
elements.sent-2411-427: We also used various metrics to relate those deformation statistics to a measure of RBC damage.
elements.sent-2411-428: The metrics that did not directly account for the history of deformation produced similar predictions as those obtained from tails of distributions.
elements.sent-2411-429: Incorporating the history in such metrics, on the other hand, may or may not change those conclusions depending on the weight given to the extreme events relative to benign conditions observed along the RBC trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-430: For a reasonable weighting, however, the earlier conclusion of the central shunt being most damaging followed by the smaller and larger mBTSs is confirmed.
elements.sent-2411-431: The damage metrics could be translated to spatial maps, thus providing a powerful tool for guiding surgical design.
elements.sent-2411-432: The maps produced through this process not only are consistent with the wall shear stress maps, but also provide a more refined quantitative measure of damage.
elements.sent-2411-433: In fact, they highlight hot zones in the shunt that coincide with the reported clinical images of shunt thrombosis on an anecdotal basis.
elements.sent-2411-434: Further refinement of the damage model and its coupling with the coagulation cascade can make the proposed multi-scale framework an indispensable tool for managing risk in the design of surgeries or medical devices.

elements.section-2411-007: Lumped parameter network: heart model and parameters
elements.sent-2411-441: Figure \ref{fig:LPNBT shows the LPN structure, composed of six main blocks, that is used in the current study (UBA: upper body artery, UBB: upper body bed, UBV: upper body vein, LBA: lower body artery, LBB: lower body bed, LBV: lower body vein, PAB: pulmonary artery bed, PAV: pulmonary artery vein, CA: coronary artery, CB: coronary bed, CV: coronary vein, LA: left atrium, RA: right atrium, and SV: single ventricle).}
elements.sent-2411-442: As observed in this figure, outside of the heart, the viscous dissipation, vessel wall distensibility, and flow inertia are modeled via linear resistors, capacitors, and inductors.
elements.sent-2411-443: Table \ref{table:LPNparam lists all the parameters of the LPN that appear in Fig. \ref{fig:LPNBT as well as their values and units.}}
elements.sent-2411-444: Due to the turbulence effects, the pressure drop across the heart valves is modeled by two non-linear resistors.
elements.sent-2411-445: The pressure of heart chambers is modeled as the sum of an active and a passive components, which for the ventricles takes the form \begin{equation}P_v = A_v \left[ E_{v_1} (V_v -V_{v_u}) + E_{v_2} (V_v -V_{v_u})^2 \right] + P_{v_0}\left(e^{K_v(V_v-V_{v_u})}-1\right), \label{eqn:P_vent}\end{equation} and for the atrium takes the form \begin{equation}P_a = A_a E_{a} (V_a -V_{a_u}) + P_{a_0}\left(e^{K_a(V_a-V_{a_u})}-1\right). \label{eqn:P_atri}\end{equation} In the above equations, $A_v$ and $A_a$ are sinusoidal functions with non-zero values during ventricle and atrium contraction, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-446: The constant value of remaining parameters, e.g., $E_{v_1 , V_v, P_{a_0 ,$ and $K_a$, is reported in Table \ref{table:LPNparam .}}}
